era_context: The Warring States period in China is in its final phase, with Qin Shihuang's forces rapidly consolidating control. In the Mediterranean, the Second Punic War is beginning (218 BCE), but in 223 BCE the region is still dominated by Hellenistic powers and Rome is consolidating its control of Italy. The Seleucid and Ptolemaic empires are major powers in the eastern Mediterranean and western Asia.

events.title: Qin forces conquer Zhao state
events.description: Qin forces, under the command of Meng Tian, conquer the Zhao state, one of the major powers of the Warring States period. Zhao, located in northern China, had been a significant military and economic power. Its conquest brings vast territories under Qin control and represents a major milestone in Qin Shihuang's unification campaign. The conquest of Zhao is followed by the conquest of Chu and other remaining states, leading to the completion of unification in 221 BCE.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian
Qin administrative records and archaeological evidence

events.certainty_note: The conquest of Zhao is attested in the Shiji and corroborated by archaeological evidence. However, the precise dating of this conquest within the 223–221 BCE period is somewhat uncertain. The Shiji's chronology is generally reliable but may have minor dating uncertainties of ±1–2 years.

events.title: Seleucus II Callinicus reigns over Seleucid Empire
events.description: Seleucus II Callinicus continues his reign over the Seleucid Empire, a period marked by internal strife and loss of eastern territories. His reign (246–222 BCE) is characterized by conflict with his brother Antiochus Hierax and the loss of control over Bactria and other eastern provinces. By 223 BCE, Seleucus II is in the final years of his reign, and the empire is weakened by these internal conflicts. His death in 222 BCE will be followed by the accession of his brother Antiochus III, who will attempt to restore Seleucid power.
events.certainty_note: Seleucus II's reign dates are well-established in Polybius and later sources. The year 223 BCE falls within his reign, which ended in 222 BCE.

events.title: Ptolemy III Euergetes reigns over Ptolemaic Egypt
events.description: Ptolemy III Euergetes continues his reign over Ptolemaic Egypt, a period of relative stability and cultural flourishing. His reign (246–221 BCE) is marked by military campaigns in Syria and Anatolia, though by 223 BCE he is in the final years of his reign. Ptolemy III is known for his patronage of the Library of Alexandria and his military campaigns against the Seleucid Empire. His death in 221 BCE will be followed by the accession of his son Ptolemy IV Philopator.
key_figures: Arsinoe II (Queen, wife of Ptolemy II; still venerated)
events.certainty_note: Ptolemy III's reign dates are well-established through papyrological evidence and Greek historical sources. The year 223 BCE falls within his reign, which ended in 221 BCE.

events.title: Second Punic War: Preparations and early campaigns
events.description: The Second Punic War (218–201 BCE) is approaching. In 223 BCE, tensions between Rome and Carthage are escalating, particularly over control of Spain. Hannibal is consolidating Carthaginian power in Spain and preparing for his famous campaign across the Alps. Roman forces are attempting to contain Carthaginian expansion. The conflict will officially begin in 218 BCE when Hannibal crosses the Alps into Italy.
events.certainty_note: The pre-war tensions and preparations are documented in classical sources, but the specific events of 223 BCE are less precisely dated. The Second Punic War officially began in 218 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the conquest of Zhao is uncertain; some sources place it in 222 BCE or 224 BCE rather than 223 BCE. The Shiji's chronology for the Warring States period is generally reliable but may have minor dating uncertainties of ±1–2 years. The precise sequence of events in the pre-war period before the Second Punic War is sometimes unclear in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: Chinese sources are dominated by the Shiji, compiled centuries after the events. Archaeological evidence from Qin-period administrative documents provides corroboration but is fragmentary. For the Mediterranean, Polybius and Livy are primary sources, both written well after the events. Egyptian sources are primarily administrative papyri. The Seleucid Empire is documented primarily through Greek and later sources. All ancient sources on the Second Punic War are written by Romans or later authors dependent on Roman sources, introducing potential bias.

graph_edges.note: Conquest of Zhao is followed by conquest of Chu in the unification campaign
Seleucus II's reign ends with his death and Antiochus III's accession
Ptolemy III's reign ends with his death and Ptolemy IV's accession
Pre-war tensions and preparations lead to the outbreak of the Second Punic War
